Entity_Description: Mission is a municipality of 41,519 residents (2021 Census) on the north bank of the Fraser River in British Columbia's Fraser Valley, about 61 km east of Vancouver. It grew from St. Mary's Mission, founded by Oblate missionaries in 1861, and is linked to Abbotsford by the Highway 11 Mission Bridge. Mission is the eastern terminus of the West Coast Express commuter rail line and is one of the few Canadian municipalities to hold its own Tree Farm Licence, reflecting a long forestry heritage. Much of its housing climbs steep benchland above the river, from older character homes to newer hillside subdivisions.

Long_Description:
Mission has always been a town that looks down at the river and up at the forest. It began as St. Mary's Mission in 1861, and today the Benedictine monks of Westminster Abbey still overlook the valley from their bell tower above Hatzic — one of the most recognizable silhouettes in the eastern Fraser Valley. Below it, the municipality of 41,519 people spreads across steep benchland rising from the north bank of the Fraser River, connected to Abbotsford by the Highway 11 bridge and to Metro Vancouver by the West Coast Express, whose commuter line ends right at Mission City station.

That geography is the first thing a {BUSINESS_TYPE} has to respect here. Unlike the flat floodplain communities across the river, Mission is a hillside town: driveways pitch, lots terrace, and the Fraser Valley's heavy winter rain doesn't pool so much as it runs — downslope, across benches, toward the river. Add the occasional outflow freeze and snow event that hits this end of the valley harder than Metro Vancouver, and {SERVICE} in Mission becomes a job about slope, drainage and access as much as anything else. {COMPANY_NAME} plans every Mission project around those realities.

The property mix is just as distinctive. Downtown and the older streets near the river carry early-1900s character homes; Cedar Valley and the benches above carry waves of newer subdivisions built as Mission became one of the Fraser Valley's growth corridors; and out along Highway 7 toward Silverdale, Silverhill, Steelhead and Hatzic Prairie, the town turns rural fast — acreages, hobby farms and properties on well and septic. Mission's economy still reflects its working roots, too: this is one of the few municipalities in Canada that manages its own Tree Farm Licence in the forests above town, and agriculture remains woven through the rural fringe.

{COMPANY_NAME} serves all of it. {SERVICE_LIST} — delivered by a team that knows the difference between a benchland lot in Cedar Valley and a floodplain acreage in Hatzic, and prices and plans accordingly. {UNIQUE_VALUE_PROP}

Whether you're near Fraser River Heritage Park, up in Steelhead, or anywhere between, {COMPANY_NAME} brings {SERVICE} that matches how Mission is actually built. Call {PHONE} to get started with a {ESTIMATE_TYPE}.

- Home to Westminster Abbey, a Benedictine monastery overlooking the Fraser Valley above Hatzic

FAQ_2_Question: How do Mission's hillside lots and winter rain affect {SERVICE}?
FAQ_2_Answer: More than most Fraser Valley towns. Because Mission is built on benchland climbing from the Fraser River, heavy winter rain runs downslope across terraced lots rather than pooling, and steep driveways can complicate access — so slope and drainage shape how we plan every job. {COMPANY_NAME} assesses each Mission property's grade and siting before work begins, which is why our {SERVICE} holds up here.
